17 min read
Just now

--

Testing a system you have never seen: notes towards an applied methodology for AI-assisted verification.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Written four days into the experiment, and reconstructed entirely from the repository’s own history: commits, lessons-learned records, findings registers, and the evolution of the AI working instructions. A snapshot of a few intense days — nothing more, and nothing less.
The starting point
A confession before the story begins: I cannot honestly claim this system was unknown to me. I am its architect. But an architect knows the blueprint — the components, the responsibilities, the intended flows, the view from ten thousand miles. The delivered system is another thing entirely. When the specifications and the software arrived, it was the first time I had seen the API: 183 endpoints of concrete, microscopic reality that the architecture had only ever implied. I had to descend from ten thousand miles to the internals, and I had days to do it, not months.
And I was, in truth, the lucky one. The independent verification and validation (IVV) contractor tasked with testing the system started from far less: a requirements document, a design justification file — a document that justifies design choices, which is not at all the same thing as describing the design — and the OpenAPI definitions. From that, they were expected to produce an automated verification suite for a four-component platform in two months. Not UI walkthroughs, which a patient human can at least improvise; automated API tests — the kind that conventional wisdom says cannot be written without detailed design documents, sequence diagrams, and better specifications than anyone ever actually receives.
So the question that started this experiment was never “can AI test a system?” It was: how on earth can I help them?
On day one, the answer began as an empty directory structure, a stack of delivered documentation, and a set of OpenAPI files. The system behind them — a data preservation and access platform built from four cooperating components — would be treated exactly as the contractor must: as an unknown system, knowable only through what was delivered on paper and over HTTP.
Four days later, the repository held over 180 catalogued scenarios, 150+ automated tests running against the live environment, and 18 evidence-backed findings — all rigorously traced back to requirements.
This article is about how. Not because the speed is the point — it isn’t — but because the speed forced a discipline. When an AI assistant can produce test code, mapping tables, and analysis documents faster than you can read them, the bottleneck of verification shifts from production to trust. Everything in this methodology is, one way or another, machinery for manufacturing trust.
Rule zero: write the constitution before the code
The first substantive artefact in the repository was not a test. It was a file calledai-working-instructions.md, and its opening rules read like commandments because they are:

• Do not invent requirements.

• Do not invent API endpoints.

• Use only the OpenAPI files as the source for executable API calls.
Anyone who has worked with a large language model knows why these rules exist. An LLM asked to test an API it half-knows will cheerfully produce plausible endpoints, plausible payloads, and plausible requirement IDs — and plausible is precisely the failure mode IVV exists to catch. So before asking the AI to do anything, we defined what it was forbidden to do, and — just as important — what the authority model was: OpenAPI is the source of truth for executable behaviour; the requirements coverage file is the source of truth for testability; the curated knowledge files are the working context; when outputs conflict with sources, stop and flag.
The instructions file turned out to be the single most important artefact in the project, for a reason we did not fully anticipate: it is versioned. Every time the collaboration failed in some way — an over-eager finding, a speculative mapping, a report that buried its conclusion — the fix was a new rule, and the rule was a commit. The methodology did not have to be designed; it accreted, one demonstrated failure at a time, in a file the AI reads at the start of every session. Refining the method and refining the prompt became the same activity.
Rule one: the repository is the deliverable
Three decisions that looked like housekeeping turned out to be strategy.
First, structure. Before any content existed, the repository had a fixed shape, and every kind of information was given exactly one home:
source-docs/original/ the delivered documents, untouched — the court of appeal
source-docs/extracted/ machine-readable extractions, regenerable by script
knowledge/ curated summaries — the AI's standard working context
openapi/ the delivered API definitions — source of truth for calls
scenarios/ scenario catalogue, wave prioritisation, test-data plan
docs/ designs, models, registers, mapping reports, meeting briefs
traceability/ generated matrices and coverage dashboards
tests/ plain pytest automation, organised by component
testdata/ reviewed, promoted test data — nothing improvised
reports/ generated evidence and execution output — never committed
campaigns/ point-in-time review snapshots: reports, lessons, conclusions
scripts/ extraction, discovery, and generation toolingThe layout encodes the epistemology. What was delivered is separated from what was derived; what was derived by script is separated from what was curated by review; what is volatile (evidence, reports) is separated from what is controlled (campaign snapshots of that same evidence, frozen at review time). A newcomer can navigate by asking “what kind of claim is this?” — and, just as importantly, so can the AI. A predictable structure is a form of prompt engineering: when every artefact type has one home, the assistant files things correctly, finds things reliably, and never has to guess which of three similar documents is authoritative.
Second, git hygiene — atomic, single-purpose commits, meaningful messages, and continuous commits. 122 commits in four days, one per reviewed step, never a day’s work compressed into “misc updates”. The reason is not tidiness. When an AI produces most of the output, the git history is the only place where the sequence of human decisions is preserved at full resolution. Read this repository’s log top to bottom, and you can watch the method being invented — extraction, then knowledge curation, then the catalogue, then waves, then the spike, then traceability, then governance, including the reversals. We used git deliberately as the knowledge-transfer medium.
History is not a byproduct of the work; history is the explanation of the work.
Third, boring technology. There is no clever testing framework here — plain pytest, markers, YAML configuration, small typed modules — and the absence is deliberate. This kind of project usually requires an elaborate custom harness; we refused because the deliverable is not artefacts; it is transferred knowledge. A contractor picking this repository up must be able to read any test in one sitting and extend it the same afternoon.
Every layer of framework cleverness would have been a tax on exactly the people we were trying to help.
The one piece of infrastructure we did allow ourselves is telling: retry with exponential backoff for transient failures. An unknown system in a shared environment will produce intermittent network noise, and a transient failure, recorded as a defect, would poison the findings register. So the client retries only what is genuinely transient, and records every retry attempt in the evidence, so that even the noise leaves an audit trail.
Phase one: feed the machine, but curate the diet
The raw inputs were DOCX and PDF documents plus OpenAPI YAML. The first working day went into extraction scripts — DOCX to Markdown, PDF to Markdown, requirements coverage to CSV/JSON, OpenAPI to endpoint inventories — so that every authoritative source existed in a machine-readable form, regenerable by a script rather than hand-copied.
Then came the step that distinguishes this approach from “paste the manual into the chat”: a curated knowledge layer. Instead of having the AI re-read a thousand pages of source material every session, we distilled the sources into a small set of reviewed files — project context, architecture summary, API surface summary, testability summary, glossary, constraints and assumptions — and made them the standard AI working context. The original documents remained in the repository for the court of appeal.
This did two things. It ensured every session started from the same reviewed understanding rather than a fresh, slightly different reading of the sources. And it created a place where human corrections stick: when we discovered that a summary was wrong, we fixed it once, rather than correcting the same misunderstanding in every future conversation.
Phase two: map the territory before touching anything
With the knowledge layer in place, the AI’s first big production task was a scenario catalogue: every end-to-end flow and supporting behaviour we could justify from the documentation, eventually 176 scenarios organised into E2E families and supporting packages. Crucially, each scenario carried metadata from birth — verification method, classification, and later requirement mappings — because we had decided that traceability would be a first-class activity, not documentation written after the fact.
Then we sorted the catalogue by danger rather than by importance. Every scenario was assigned to a wave:

• Wave 0 — readiness: is the environment reachable, do credentials work, do health endpoints answer?

• Wave 1 — read-only and demonstrably non-state-changing operations.

• Wave 2 — state-changing but non-destructive operations.

• Wave 3 — destructive, simulator-dependent, or timing-dependent scenarios, disabled by default and excluded from every default test run.
The wave model is the safety answer to an uncomfortable question:
What does “be careful” mean when an AI writes tests against a live shared environment?
Our answer:
It means the default action is always the harmless one.
Destructive tests require explicit markers, dedicated test data, and a governance decision. Nothing state-changing runs by accident. When we later promoted three POST endpoints from Wave 2 to Wave 1, it was because we could demonstrate — not assume — that they changed no state; the rationale for the reclassification is itself a recorded decision.
Phase three: first contact, one controlled step at a time
Wave 0 ran on day two: six readiness tests against the live environment. Unglamorous, and exactly the point — the first automated contact with an unknown system should be the contact that cannot break anything.
Test data followed the same discipline. We never hardcoded a product ID or bucket name that the AI had found somewhere in the documentation. Instead, every required datum became a numbered item (TD-01 to TD-35) in a readiness plan, with a lifecycle: discovered by read-only scripts against the live system, validated with evidence, and only then promoted to a reviewed test-data profile that tests may consume. Tests that need unpromoted data skip, cleanly and visibly, rather than improvising.
The climax of this phase was deliberately small. On day four, we ran a single controlled experiment — an end-to-end customisation spike — whose plan is worth quoting, because it captures the mindset:
“The objective is not merely to discover a bucket and object. The objective is to establish a repeatable workflow capable of generating realistic test artefacts on demand. The workflow itself is the primary deliverable.”
One customisation order, assembled entirely from promoted and validated pieces, was submitted to the ordering component. It was accepted (HTTP 201, auto-approved
